title: FIFA World Cup Group A Winner
description:
This market will resolve according to the team that wins Group A in the 2026 FIFA World Cup group stage, scheduled for June 11-27, 2026.

If multiple teams tie as group winners, this market will resolve according to the official tiebreak procedure of the 2026 FIFA World Cup.

If the World Cup group stage is cancelled, postponed after September 30, 2026, or there is otherwise no winner declared for this group within that timeframe, this market will resolve to “Other”.

The primary resolution source for this market will be official information from FIFA (https://www.fifa.com/); however, a consensus of credible reporting may also be used.

markets.description:
This market will resolve according to the team that wins Group A in the 2026 FIFA World Cup group stage, scheduled for June 11-27, 2026.

eventMetadata.context_description: Mexico's trader consensus lead at 50.5% implied probability stems from co-host advantages, including the June 11 opener at Azteca Stadium against South Africa, bolstered by captain Edson Álvarez and Hirving Lozano's high-intensity training camps under Javier Aguirre despite goalkeeper Luis Malagón's Achilles injury sidelining him. Czechia's 23.5% reflects dramatic UEFA playoff qualification via penalties over Ireland and Tomáš Souček's midfield leadership, positioning them as a disciplined dark horse. South Korea's 19.5% holds despite Son Heung-min's form, tempered by recent friendly losses to Austria and Ivory Coast's 4-0 rout. South Africa's 4.7% underscores their underdog status as first-timers since 2010, with mixed Panama results highlighting limited big-stage experience in this competitive group.

eventMetadata.context_description: Switzerland leads Group B trader consensus at 55.5% implied probability to win the group, driven by their top FIFA ranking (19th), defensive solidity, and consistent UEFA qualifier performances under coach Murat Yakin, featuring midfield anchor Granit Xhaka. Canada's 24.5% reflects co-host advantages with home support in Toronto and Vancouver matches, plus attacking pace from Alphonso Davies and Jonathan David amid improved CONCACAF form. Bosnia and Herzegovina's 20.5% stems from fresh momentum after their March playoff penalty shootout upset over Italy to qualify, injecting underdog energy despite inconsistent results. Qatar trails at 2.0%, hampered by dismal 2022 World Cup group exit as hosts and weaker AFC standing, with no recent developments shifting dynamics in the past week.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus heavily favors Brazil at 76.5% to win Group C, underpinned by their No. 6 FIFA ranking, five World Cup titles, and attacking depth featuring Vinícius Júnior and Raphinha, despite recent injury blows including Rodrygo's ACL tear, Estêvão's severe hamstring strain, and Éder Militão's thigh issues announced May 7. Morocco holds 17.5% implied probability thanks to their 2022 semifinal heroics, No. 8 ranking, perfect CAF qualifying record, and counterattacking prowess in March friendlies over Paraguay and Ecuador, though Achraf Hakimi's hamstring strain poses a concern. Scotland (5.1%, ~51st-ranked) and Haiti (0.3%, 83rd-ranked) lag with modest recent qualifier form and historic returns to the tournament, positioning them as longshots for advancement.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus favors the USA at 42.5% implied probability to win Group D, bolstered by co-host home advantage across multiple venues and recent 2-1 friendly victories over Paraguay and Australia last year, despite a 2-1 loss to Türkiye in June. Türkiye's 34.5% reflects their robust UEFA qualifying runner-up finish behind Spain—despite a 6-0 home defeat—and technical midfield stars like Hakan Çalhanoğlu and Arda Güler, now in intensive pre-tournament camps. Paraguay holds 18.5% on defensive organization under Gustavo Alfaro, though injury worries persist; Australia trails at 7.3% amid long-haul travel, key absences like Callum Elder's knee surgery, and recent USMNT prep losses to Belgium (5-2) and Portugal (2-0) tightening the race.

eventMetadata.context_description: Germany's 71.5% implied probability to win Group E stems from their top-five FIFA ranking, Julian Nagelsmann's high-pressing 4-2-3-1 system anchored by Joshua Kimmich and Jamal Musiala, and unmatched depth despite Serge Gnabry's May 7 adductor injury ruling him out of the tournament—prompting a squad announcement delay to May 21 for recovery assessments. Ecuador's 18% trader consensus reflects their resilient CONMEBOL qualifiers, including a 1-0 upset over Argentina on March 31 and a 1-1 friendly draw with Netherlands, enabling a compact low-block to challenge for second ahead of Ivory Coast's athletic counters bolstered by Sébastien Haller's full fitness. Curaçao trails at 0.9% as the smallest nation ever to qualify via unbeaten Concacaf play, posing limited upset threat amid intense pre-tournament camps.

title: Which continent will win the 2026 Fifa World Cup?
description:
This market will resolve to the continent of the country that wins the 2026 FIFA World Cup, currently scheduled for June 11-July 19, 2026.

For example, if France wins the tournament, the market will resolve to Europe.

If the 2026 FIFA World Cup is cancelled, postponed after December 31, 2026, or there is otherwise no winner declared within that timeframe, this market will resolve to “Other”.

The definitive source of the continent for each country will be World Population Review (https://worldpopulationreview.com/country-rankings/list-of-countries-by-continent).

The primary resolution sources for this market will be official information from FIFA (https://www.fifa.com/) and the World Population Review; however, a consensus of credible reporting may also be used.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus prices Europe at 71.5% implied probability to win the FIFA World Cup 2026, reflecting unmatched squad depth across 16 qualified UEFA nations including top-ranked France (#1), Euro 2024 champions Spain (#2), England (#4), and Portugal (#5), bolstered by dominant qualification campaigns and recent FIFA rankings through April. South America's 20.5% stems from defending champions Argentina (#3) and Brazil's pedigree despite just six CONMEBOL slots, while expanded slots aided Africa (nine teams like Algeria, Egypt) and Asia (nine AFC qualifiers including Japan, South Korea) at 3.5% and 3.4%, lacking deep knockout experience. Qualification wrapped March 31 with Europe's playoff wins for Sweden, Turkey, Bosnia & Herzegovina, and Czechia, cementing their edge ahead of the June 11 group stage kickoff; North America's hosts and Oceania trail as longshots.

title: World Cup game relocated away from Mexico?
description:
This market will resolve to “Yes” if it is announced that any 2026 FIFA World Cup match originally scheduled to be held in Mexico will be relocated to a location outside of Mexico by June 10, 2026, 11:59 ET. Otherwise, it will resolve to “No”.

A qualifying announcement will immediately resolve the market to "Yes" regardless of whether the relocation is later revoked.

The resolution source will be official information from FIFA; however, a consensus of credible reporting may also be used.

markets.question: World Cup game relocated away from Mexico?
markets.description:
This market will resolve to “Yes” if it is announced that any 2026 FIFA World Cup match originally scheduled to be held in Mexico will be relocated to a location outside of Mexico by June 10, 2026, 11:59 ET. Otherwise, it will resolve to “No”.

A qualifying announcement will immediately resolve the market to "Yes" regardless of whether the relocation is later revoked.

The resolution source will be official information from FIFA; however, a consensus of credible reporting may also be used.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus at 96.8% "No" stems from FIFA's firm confirmation of all original Mexican venues for the 2026 World Cup, including Estadio Azteca's opening fixture on June 11 despite ongoing renovations that drew early-year concerns. February-March rumors of relocations—sparked by cartel violence near Guadalajara and tight timelines at Azteca, Akron, and BBVA stadiums—were swiftly dismissed by FIFA statements emphasizing logistical impossibilities this close to kickoff. Recent developments show smooth preparations, like Mexico advancing school holidays to June 5 for fan access, with no official changes amid three host cities set for 13 group stage and knockout matches. Unrealistic but possible shifts could arise from a stadium catastrophe or pre-tournament security crisis.

title: Which team will replace Iran at FIFA World Cup?
description:
This market will resolve in favor of the men’s national team that is confirmed by FIFA to replace the Football Federation Islamic Republic of Iran (IR) at the 2026 FIFA World Cup.

If an unlisted men’s national team is officially announced to replace Iran at the 2026 FIFA World Cup, this market will resolve to “Other”

If the 2026 FIFA World Cup is cancelled, postponed after December 31 2026, 11:59 PM ET, or there is no confirmation that any team will replace Iran within that timeframe, the corresponding market will resolve to “No Replacement”.

The primary resolution source for this market will be information from FIFA (https://www.fifa.com/), however a consensus of credible reporting will also be used.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus heavily favors no replacement for Iran at the 2026 FIFA World Cup, reflecting FIFA president Gianni Infantino's April 30 confirmation that Iran will participate despite geopolitical tensions and earlier withdrawal signals from Iran's sports minister amid regional conflict. Italian officials dismissed a U.S. envoy's proposal to slot in the four-time champions as "shameful," while FIFA rejected Iran's bid to relocate U.S.-based matches to Mexico, affirming logistical plans. UAE edges as the next AFC playoff contender behind qualified teams like Iraq, explaining its slim odds. Only escalating security issues, formal withdrawal, or unprecedented FIFA intervention could shift this dominant positioning.
